comments.author: @danepikkola9119
comments.text: From 1992 to '94 I was with the Peace Corps teaching at Masaphula high school on the southern border. Much here is accurate, but the AI narration is a real turn off. Much of the pronunciation is not correct. It is true that Eswatini (will always be Swaziland for me) is unique in Africa, and I highly recommend anyone visit. Don't get me started on the monarchy, however. It was life changing to live there amongst regular people on my homestead in Makhosisi, and I am eternally grateful for my experience with my adopted family. There is so much more to tell.  Ngiyabonga Eswatini. Vusi Mhlanga.

comments.author: @UHURU-001
comments.text: Eswatini (or the landlocked kingdom of Swaziland) is one of my favourite places on Earth.  I spent months exploring those mountains in the west, bordering the Barberton mountainlands and the Kruger National Park in South Africa, where some of the oldest rocks on the planet are found.   Another favourite place for me is Lesotho (or the landlocked kingdom of Basutoland) and the magnificent Drakensberg Mountains on the border with Kwa Zulu Natal, South Africa, A UNESCO World Heritage reserve.

comments.author: @TerraVista-4K
comments.text: The video highlights how Eswatini’s absolute monarchy preserves its identity, offering a unique balance through the 'dual monarchy' system (the King and Queen Mother ruling together). But frankly speaking, in the 21st century, is concentrating absolute, unchallenged authority in the hands of a single monarch truly sustainable for the country's future? Is this cultural stability something the people genuinely want to preserve, or is it just because they lack a democratic mechanism to voice opposing views?

replies.author: @SifisoMamba-iq2db
replies.text: Life is good and simple here. There is direction and purpose. The monarchy is not on the way of progress. Not perfect by all means.

replies.author: @BestTravelDocumentary4k
replies.text: That is a very thought-provoking perspective on the country's future.

comments.author: @HoangKien-p3s
comments.text: The details of a white rhino viewing hike in Hlane National Park costing only $14 truly shocked me. So cheap for an invaluable experience! The words of ranger Musa: "We are not protecting rhinos for ourselves, but for future generations..." were deeply moving. Eswatini's strict anti-hunting laws deserve to be emulated by the whole world.

replies.author: @BestTravelDocumentary4k
replies.text: Thanks for your thoughtful comment.

comments.author: @LMDiscoveryGlobe
comments.text: The way modern life and ancient tradition blend here is magical. My local friends would be working on laptops in the city on Friday, and back in their grandmother's traditional beehive hut by Saturday.

replies.author: @BestTravelDocumentary4k
replies.text: Thanks for sharing that beautiful observation.

replies.author: @revothomas9343
replies.text: @m@michelfauteux4500spect other people’s cultures and traditions,it’s not like you really care about people of Eswatini, you will be surprised that these people are supporting their monarchy, it’s you the outsiders that like to poke their nose on other people’s affairs.The owner of the documentary made it clear that this is a about the people not monarchy and you continue with your microaggression agenda , just stop it doesn’t work anymore it’s 2026.

replies.author: @michelfauteux4500
replies.text: @revothomas9343 Simply put, the disparities between common folk and the monarchs are much more striking in Africa. You could say that European royalty in past centuries was even worse. But as is pointed out in the video, Eswatini is the last remaining monarchy on the continent. Moreover, there are other forms of exploitation perpetrated by by non-royal politicians.

replies.author: @BestTravelDocumentary4k
replies.text: The focus of this film was to highlight the daily lives of the local people.

replies.author: @revothomas9343
replies.text: But the English & Europeans monarchy,s enjoy the worldly extravagances, why is it pointed out when it’s African monarchy, it’s also their culture.
